event: Saint Agnes Standing in Triumph over Martyrdom
symbols: palm branch in her left hand — the universal symbol of martyrdom and victory over death, triumph of faith through suffering
right hand placed over heart — the gesture of interior devotion and purity, total consecration of self to God
lamb at the base — the attribute of Saint Agnes (from Latin agnus), typologically linking her to Christ the Lamb of God
flowing drapery in heavy folds — dignity and modesty of the virgin saint, the clothing of righteousness
youthful figure with serene downcast gaze — calm courage of faith before execution, the peace that surpasses understanding
description: A bronze sculpture of a standing young woman in flowing classical robes. She holds a palm branch in her left hand while her right hand rests over her heart. Her head is slightly inclined with a serene contemplative expression. The drapery is richly modeled in deep heavy folds cascading from her shoulders to the rectangular base. At her feet partially obscured by fabric a small lamb rests on the ground. The figure stands in a gentle contrapposto pose conveying both grace and quiet resolve. The dark bronze patina gives the work a solemn dignified character.
theological_description: This bronze of Saint Agnes embodies one of the earliest and most beloved martyrdom narratives of the Christian Church. Agnes, traditionally a twelve- or thirteen-year-old Roman girl executed during the Diocletian persecution around AD 304, became the paradigmatic figure of virginal fidelity to Christ unto death. The palm branch she holds is the ancient martyr's attribute: 'The righteous flourish like the palm tree' (Psalm 92:12), and in Revelation the white-robed multitude who came through the great tribulation stand before the throne 'with palm branches in their hands' (Revelation 7:9). The lamb at her feet operates on multiple levels: her personal iconographic attribute (playing on Latin agnus), but simultaneously linking her to Christ, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world (John 1:29). Agnes is a lamb who follows the Lamb — a virgin who kept herself undefiled and follows the Lamb wherever he goes (Revelation 14:4). Her hand upon her heart signals the interior consecration that martyrdom merely makes visible. Ambrose celebrated Agnes as proof that faithfulness is not a function of age but of the Spirit's power: 'My power is made perfect in weakness' (2 Corinthians 12:9). She is rendered not in agony but in serenity — the peace of one who has passed through death into life, reflecting Paul's assurance: 'For to me to live is Christ, and to die is gain' (Philippians 1:21).

scripture_refs.reason: The great multitude with palm branches — Agnes holds the palm of martyrdom, numbered among those who came through the great tribulation
Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world — the lamb at her feet links her typologically to Christ's sacrificial identity
Those who follow the Lamb wherever he goes — Agnes as virgin martyr who follows Christ the Lamb
The righteous flourish like the palm tree — the palm branch as biblical symbol of the righteous flourishing through suffering
My power is made perfect in weakness — the young girl's martyrdom demonstrates divine strength through human frailty
To live is Christ and to die is gain — Agnes's serene countenance reflects conviction that death for Christ is triumph
Be faithful unto death and I will give you the crown of life — the promise to those who endure persecution
